title: Death rate from neonatal preterm birth complications
subtitle: The estimated death rate from [neonatal](#dod:neonatal) [preterm birth complications](#dod:preterm-birth-complications) in children under five years old, per 100,000.
caption:
Death rate from neonatal preterm birth complications, 2023
The estimated death rate from neonatal preterm birth complications in children under five years old, per 100,000.

This choropleth map displays the estimated death rate from neonatal preterm birth complications in children under five years old, measured per 100,000 population for the year 2023. The visualization utilizes a continuous color scale ranging from pale yellow, representing rates near zero, to dark navy blue, indicating rates approaching 1,000 deaths per 100,000. The geographic distribution shows the highest mortality rates concentrated across sub-Saharan Africa and parts of South Asia. Countries in West and Central Africa, including Nigeria, Mali, and the Democratic Republic of the Congo, appear in the darkest blue shades, placing them in the upper bands of the scale between 200 and 1,000 deaths per 100,000. India and Pakistan also display medium-to-dark blue tones, signifying elevated rates relative to the global average. In contrast, the lowest death rates are visible across Western and Northern Europe, Australia, and North America, where nations such as the United States, Canada, France, and the United Kingdom are shaded in pale yellow or light green, corresponding to values below 20 per 100,000. Russia and China fall into the light green category, indicating low-to-moderate rates compared to the highest burden regions. Greenland is marked with a hatched pattern, denoting that no data is available for that territory. The data is age-standardized to allow for comparisons between countries and over time. The map presents a clear divide where the burden of death from preterm birth complications is heavily skewed toward the African continent and South Asia, while remaining minimal in high-income regions of the Americas, Europe, and Oceania.
